soap:
REVIEW · lance-format/lance#7498 — fix(arrow): preserve inner nulls in convert_to_floating_point
LuciferYang (core) · M (+69/-12, 1 file)

ONE-LINER
  FixedSizeListArrayExt::convert_to_floating_point built each integer arm's
  float values with from_iter_values(...filter_map(|x| x.map(...))), which
  DROPS null inner elements — shortening the values buffer while the outer
  FixedSizeList null buffer stays full-length. For a nullable integer vector
  that panics in FixedSizeListArray::new (surviving count not a multiple of
  list size) or silently shifts every later element into the wrong list. The
  fix switches each arm to from_iter(...map(|x| x.map(...))), which keeps the
  Option<T> stream so length and null positions are preserved.

SUBJECTIVE (author's framing)
  Focused correctness fix to the vector-indexing conversion helper. Author
  explicitly scopes out collapsing the match to arrow_cast::cast (would add an
  arrow-cast dep) — reasonable, kept separate.

OBJECTIVE (what I verified against ground truth)
  • lib.rs:283-411 (fetched at head acbf746) — read the WHOLE match, not just
    the diff. Six integer arms changed: Int8/Int16/Int32 -> Float32,
    Int64/UInt8/UInt32 -> Float64. The supported-type set is UNCHANGED; only
    null handling per arm changed. No arm was missed — UInt16/Int*-others/
    UInt64 were never supported and still fall to the error arm (lib.rs:401).
  • The primitive choice is correct. from_iter_values consumes T and the
    .filter_map unwraps Option, discarding None -> short, all-valid values
    buffer paired with a full-length outer null buffer = the bug. from_iter
    consumes Option<T>, so the new Float array has the same length and a
    validity buffer matching the inner element nulls. into_iter() on a
    PrimitiveArray yields Option<T> reflecting that array's own nulls — correct
    source. self.nulls().cloned() (outer/list-level validity) is untouched and
    still correct; the two validity levels are distinct and both now consistent.
  • For all-non-null input the new code is value-for-value identical to the old
    (no None in the stream), so existing callers feeding dense vectors are
    unaffected — confirmed by construction, not just asserted.
  • Callers (code search, 6 hits): residual.rs, dot.rs, ivf.rs, kmeans.rs,
    cosine.rs, l2.rs — exactly the vector-indexing path the body names. None
    relied on the drop-nulls behavior (it was a latent panic/corruption, not a
    contract).
  • Tests: test_convert_to_floating_point_preserves_inner_nulls (Int8->f32,
    lib.rs:1566) and ..._f64_arm (UInt8->f64) cover BOTH branch families. Each
    asserts len()==2 lists / 4 values with the null kept at position 1 and
    neighbours intact — directly pins the shift/panic regression. The f32 test
    panics on main (3 surviving values, 3 % 2 != 0), passes with fix. Adequate.
  • could not read: none — 1/1 files in packet, 0 omitted; full function and
    callers cross-checked against GitHub at head.

ASSESSMENT
  risk: low (refined DOWN from baseline med with reason) · urgency: high
    — Single-file, mechanical, behavior-preserving for the non-null case and
    strictly-corrective for the null case. The only "risk" axis here is
    IO/data-correctness, and the change moves it from broken to correct. No
    unsafe, no concurrency, no wire/format change. Baseline med was path-only
    (core lib); content does not support holding it at med.
  escalation: none (forced=false, no rules)
  CI: green · mergeable: true

DRAFT COMMENTS
  praise(non-blocking): Clean root-cause fix — from_iter vs from_iter_values is
    exactly the right lever, and covering both the f32 and f64 arm families in
    tests pins the regression precisely.
  question(non-blocking): Now that inner nulls survive into the Float arrays fed
    to l2/dot/cosine/kmeans, do those distance kernels handle inner-null float
    elements as intended (e.g. NaN-propagation vs skip), or was the previous
    behavior masking that path entirely? Not gating this fix — the old behavior
    was a panic/corruption — but worth confirming there isn't a second latent
    issue downstream. Does not block.

RECOMMENDATION: approve
  confidence: high · what would make this wrong: if some caller actually
  depended on convert_to_floating_point compacting out nulls (it does not —
  that path panicked), or if a distance kernel downstream now mis-handles the
  newly-preserved inner nulls (flagged as a non-blocking question; separate
  from this fix's correctness). 0 blocking conditions.
